title: Custard tart
subtitle: Baked egg custard in a shortcrust case, finished with nutmeg
excerpt: A classic English custard tart: a blind-baked shortcrust case filled with a baked egg and cream custard, grated with fresh nutmeg. The custard sets to a trembling, smooth consistency that slices cleanly when cold.
sourceNotes: Baked custard tarts appear in British cookery from the medieval period, where they were known as daryols or custards. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, gives baked egg custard recipes consistent with the modern form. The English custard tart differs from the Portuguese pastel de nata in using a thinner, less caramelised filling baked at a lower temperature, which produces a smoother, paler set. Fresh nutmeg grated over the top is a consistent feature across historical British recipes.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before and refrigerate overnight; the custard sets firmer and the pastry stays crisp.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, for the custard filling; pastry blind-bakes at 180°C fan

glossaryTerms.definition: Baking a pastry case empty before adding the filling. The case is lined with baking paper and filled with baking beans or uncooked rice to hold the pastry down and stop it puffing up. Blind-baking fully sets the base so the custard can go in wet without soaking through and making the pastry soggy.

content.text: into a fully set case or it will soak the pastry from the inside. Bake the filling at 150°C fan: any hotter and the eggs scramble rather than set to a smooth gel. The custard is ready when the edges are set and the centre has a gentle wobble, like barely set jelly. It firms up considerably in the fridge. Resist cutting until fully cold.

Rub the cold butter into the flour and icing sugar until fine crumbs form. Add the egg yolk and cold water; mix to a dough. Flatten, wrap, and refrigerate for 20 minutes. Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Roll the pastry to 3 mm on a lightly floured surface and line a 23 cm tart tin, pressing into the edges. Refrigerate for 10 minutes. Line the chilled case with scrunched baking paper, fill with baking beans or rice, and blind-bake for 15 minutes. Remove the paper and beans and bake for a further 5 minutes until the base is pale gold. Reduce the oven to 150°C fan.

Whisk the egg yolks and caster sugar until smooth. Warm the milk and double cream together in a small saucepan until steaming but not boiling. Pour slowly over the egg mixture, whisking. Add the vanilla. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve into a jug. Place the tart case on a baking tray on the oven shelf. Pour the custard in to near the top. Grate nutmeg generously over the surface. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes, until the edges are set and the centre has a gentle wobble. Cool in the tin, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours before unmoulding and slicing.
